textobject.phrase: opcgw — ChirpStack to OPC UA Gateway

bookinfo.title: opcgw User Manual
bookinfo.subtitle: ChirpStack LoRaWAN to OPC UA Gateway

legalnotice.para: This work is licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0 license.

revision.revremark: Initial release of opcgw user manual

Comprehensive update covering Epics 2-6: SQLite-backed persistence (WAL mode, connection pool), command queue with delivery confirmation, gRPC pagination, support for all metric types, gateway health metrics in OPC UA, stale-data status codes (Good/Uncertain/Bad), structured logging with microsecond timestamps + correlation IDs, configurable verbosity, performance-budget warnings, and remote-diagnostic operation taxonomy. Configuration chapter rewritten against actual TOML keys; new chapters on Logging & Observability and Operational Monitoring; new appendices for configuration reference, OPC UA address space, and operation names.

v2.0 GA Release. Adds Epic 7 (OPC UA security hardening: three-endpoint matrix, NFR9 private-key permission enforcement, max_connections session cap, opcua_auth_failed and opcua_session_count_at_limit audit events); Epic 8 (real-time subscriptions with DataChangeFilter, configurable subscription and message-size limits, OPC UA HistoryRead and max_history_data_results_per_node); Epic 9 (embedded Axum Web UI behind HTTP Basic auth, dashboard and live metrics page, configuration hot-reload via SIGHUP, CRUD endpoints for applications, devices, and commands); Epic A (storage payload migration closing issue #108 — payload-bearing MetricType, v007 and v008 SQLite migrations, NaN/Inf poller filter, real measurement values round-tripped end-to-end for the first time). New Installation, Upgrade and Migration chapters; Configuration chapter extended for [opcua].max_connections, [web], and the audit-event reason taxonomy. Docker Hub (docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw) and GHCR mirror (ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw) publishing documented; Dockerfile pinned to ubuntu:24.04 and runs as non-root opcgw user (UID 10001).

abstract.para: opcgw is a Rust-based gateway that bridges ChirpStack LoRaWAN Network Server with OPC UA clients for industrial automation and SCADA systems. It polls device metrics from ChirpStack at configurable intervals, persists them in a local SQLite database (WAL mode for concurrent reads), exposes them via OPC UA 1.04 with stale-data status codes, real-time subscriptions, and HistoryRead, and accepts downlink commands from OPC UA clients with end-to-end delivery confirmation. An embedded Axum web UI (opt-in) renders the gateway's live status and per-metric values for operator triage. As of v2.0 GA (Epic A), the gateway persists real typed measurement payloads end-to-end. This manual provides comprehensive guidance on installation, configuration, operation, observability, troubleshooting, and upgrade / migration.

section.title: What is opcgw?
section.para: opcgw (OPC Gateway) is a middleware application that bridges the gap between LoRaWAN sensor networks and industrial automation systems. It provides bidirectional integration between:

listitem.para: ChirpStack LoRaWAN Network Server — Manages LoRaWAN devices, gathers uplink metrics from distributed IoT sensors, and accepts downlink commands.
OPC UA Clients — SCADA systems, industrial controllers, and human-machine interfaces that consume metrics in real time and issue commands.

section.para: The gateway continuously polls device metrics from ChirpStack, persists them in a local SQLite database with WAL-mode concurrency, exposes the current values plus gateway health as OPC UA variables, and queues outbound commands for reliable delivery.

listitem.para: Real-time data collection — Polls metrics from ChirpStack at configurable intervals; supports counter, gauge, and absolute metric kinds plus the boolean, integer, float, and string OPC UA types. As of Epic A (v2.0 GA) real measurement payloads are persisted and round-tripped end-to-end — the pre-Epic-A discriminant-string bug (issue #108) is closed.

Persistent storage — SQLite-backed current-value table (UPSERT semantics) plus an append-only metric_history table for historical analysis. WAL mode plus a per-task connection pool eliminates the prior in-memory Mutex bottleneck. Schema is at v008 (Epic A typed-payload columns with exactly-one-non-NULL CHECK constraint).

Reliable command delivery — A SQLite-backed FIFO command queue with parameter validation, ChirpStack downlink integration, and three-state delivery tracking (Pending → Sent → Confirmed / Failed) with configurable confirmation timeout.

OPC UA 1.04 server — Native endpoint built on async-opcua exposing every configured metric plus a synthetic Gateway folder containing health metrics (LastPollTimestamp, error_count, chirpstack_available). Three endpoints are advertised concurrently on the same TCP port: None / None (development), Basic256 / Sign, and Basic256 / SignAndEncrypt (production default).

OPC UA subscriptions and HistoryRead — Real-time monitored-item subscriptions (DataChangeFilter-aware so status-only transitions are surfaced) and OPC UA HistoryRead against the metric_history table. The per-call response cap is [opcua].max_history_data_results_per_node (default 10000 rows ≈ 28 h at the default 10 s polling cadence); SCADA clients page via follow-up calls (manual paging — OPC UA continuation points are not yet implemented).

Hardened OPC UA security — Single user/password authentication with a sanitised audit trail (event="opcua_auth_failed"), configurable session cap ([opcua].max_connections, default 10, hard cap 4096) with at-limit audit (event="opcua_session_count_at_limit"), and NFR9-enforced filesystem permissions on the OPC UA private key (the gateway refuses to start unless pki/private/*.pem is mode 0o600 and the parent directory is 0o700).

Embedded Web UI (opt-in) — An Axum HTTP server gated by HTTP Basic auth surfaces the gateway's live status dashboard and a per-metric values page. Disabled by default ([web].enabled = false); credentials are shared with [opcua] so one rotation step covers both surfaces. Stories 9-4 / 9-5 / 9-6 add CRUD endpoints for applications, devices, and commands; Story 9-7 wires SIGHUP-driven configuration hot-reload.

Stale-data status codes — Each OPC UA read returns a status code derived from the metric's age: Good (within threshold), Uncertain (older but within 24 h), or Bad (older than 24 h). Operators see freshness without parsing timestamps.

Graceful degradation — Continues operating when ChirpStack is unreachable, when SQLite reports SQLITE_BUSY, when a poll cycle partially fails, or when individual devices return errors. Per-cycle error counts and outage signals are surfaced in logs and via OPC UA health metrics.

Structured observability — All log output is structured (key=value), uses microsecond UTC timestamps, and carries a per-OPC-UA-read request_id that propagates through storage and staleness checks. Per-operation budget warnings fire when opc_ua_read > 100 ms, storage_query > 10 ms, or batch_write > 500 ms.

Configurable verbosity — The global log level is controlled by the CLI -d count, the OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL environment variable, or the [logging].level TOML key (precedence in that order). Per-module log files capture at TRACE independently for post-mortem analysis.
gRPC pagination — Large device fleets are paginated through the ChirpStack List APIs with a configurable page size, preventing single-RPC overflows.
Cancellation-aware shutdown — The poller, OPC UA server, and pruner all share a CancellationToken; Ctrl-C (or SIGTERM) initiates a graceful drain with bounded latency.

section.title: System Architecture
section.para: opcgw follows a modular, message-passing architecture with a shared SQLite-backed storage layer. The poller, OPC UA server, and pruner each hold their own Arc<dyn StorageBackend> handle and coordinate via SQLite WAL mode rather than a Rust mutex.

listitem.para: Poller module (src/chirpstack.rs) — Authenticates to the ChirpStack gRPC API, polls metrics on a fixed cadence, classifies the metric kind (counter/gauge/absolute/unknown), validates values against the configured target type, and writes the cycle's metrics to SQLite as a single batched transaction. Tracks per-cycle error counts and emits a chirpstack_outage warning on the first connectivity failure of a cycle.

Storage layer (src/storage/) — Abstracted behind the StorageBackend trait with two implementations: SqliteBackend (production) and InMemoryBackend (test-only). The SQLite implementation uses a connection pool so each async task gets its own rusqlite::Connection, enabling concurrent readers and a single writer at the database level. Schema migrations run at startup. SQLite errors classified as DatabaseBusy or DatabaseLocked emit a storage_query warning labelled SQLITE_BUSY with a sibling sqlite_error_code field for differentiation.

OPC UA server (src/opc_ua.rs) — Builds the address space from the [[application]] sections in the configuration file, exposes each metric as a Variant with a staleness-derived StatusCode, and exposes the synthetic Gateway folder. Per-metric staleness transitions are tracked in a DashMap<(device_id, metric_name), StatusCode> for lock-free concurrent reads. Each read is wrapped in an info_span! so downstream storage_query and staleness_check logs inherit a request_id for correlation.

Configuration system (src/config.rs) — TOML-based (figment) with environment-variable overrides using the OPCGW_ prefix and __ as the nested-key separator. A two-phase init reads the [logging] block before tracing initialises so log-directory and verbosity decisions reach the file appender from the very first message.

Logging system (src/main.rs) — tracing subscriber with a console layer (stderr) plus five daily-rolling file appenders: a root opc_ua_gw.log and four per-module files (chirpstack.log, opc_ua.log, storage.log, config.log). All formatted with microsecond UTC timestamps via ChronoUtc.

Command queue (src/command_validation.rs + storage layer) — OPC UA write requests are validated against the [[application.device.command]] schemas, persisted to a SQLite-backed FIFO queue, dequeued by the poller, sent to ChirpStack via downlink, and tracked through delivery confirmation with a configurable timeout.

Embedded web server (src/web/) — Optional Axum HTTP server (opt-in via [web].enabled = true) sharing [opcua].user_name / [opcua].user_password credentials behind HTTP Basic auth. Renders a live status dashboard (GET /), per-metric values page (GET /metrics.html), and CRUD endpoints for applications / devices / commands (/api/applications/*). Source IP is carried directly via Axum's ConnectInfo extractor in audit events.

Configuration hot-reload (src/config_reload.rs) — On SIGHUP, the gateway re-reads config/config.toml through the same figment chain used at startup, validates the candidate, classifies the diff (hot-reload-safe vs restart-required vs address-space-mutating), and atomically swaps the live Arc<AppConfig>. Audit events: event="config_reload_attempted", config_reload_succeeded, config_reload_failed. See Story 9-7 limits in .

section.para: Data flow:
section.programlisting: ChirpStack gRPC API │ poll ▼ ChirpstackPoller ──── batch_write_metrics() ──▶ SqliteBackend (WAL) │ │ get_metric_value() ▼ OpcUa Server ──▶ OPC UA clients ▲ │ │ queue_command() │ writes ◀─────────────────┘

listitem.para: Any 64-bit processor (x86-64, ARM64). Two or more cores recommended for production so the poller, OPC UA server, and storage pruner can run on independent tokio worker threads.
Minimum 512 MB RAM; 2 GB recommended for deployments with 100+ devices. The SQLite WAL plus per-module log buffering dominate memory usage at steady state; per-metric overhead is negligible.

Minimum 100 MB free disk for the binary, configuration, and a small SQLite database. Allocate at least 1 GB for production deployments retaining 30 days of historical metrics with a 10 second polling interval. Log files (one root plus four per-module daily-rolling files) typically add 10-100 MB/day depending on the configured verbosity.
Outbound TCP to the ChirpStack gRPC endpoint (typically port 8080) and inbound TCP for OPC UA clients (default port 4855).
OS — Linux (Debian, Ubuntu, CentOS, Alpine), macOS, or Windows with Docker. The reference deployment uses Linux containers.

Runtime options — three supported paths: a published Docker image (docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw:2.0 primary; ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw:2.0 mirror; multi-arch linux/amd64 + linux/arm64), a systemd-managed binary, or a from-source build. See .
Rust toolchain — version 1.94.0 or later when building from source. The release binary and the published Docker image have no Rust runtime requirement.

Build prerequisites (from-source only) — protobuf-compiler, libssl-dev, pkg-config on Debian/Ubuntu; equivalent packages on other distributions. The build script (build.rs) compiles the ChirpStack protobuf definitions via tonic-build.
SQLite — bundled via rusqlite's bundled feature; no separate SQLite installation is needed at runtime. The sqlite3 CLI is recommended on the operator host for the migration pre-flight script ().
ChirpStack — version 4.0 or later with the gRPC API enabled and an API token issued for the target tenant.
Firewall — outbound to ChirpStack (gRPC), inbound to OPC UA clients (port 4855 by default), and inbound to the embedded web UI (default port 8080) if [web].enabled = true.

chapter.title: Installation
chapter.para: Three supported installation paths: a published Docker image (from Docker Hub or the GHCR mirror, with an optional Docker Compose recipe), a systemd-managed binary, or a from-source build. Post-install verification is the same regardless of path.

section.title: Docker (Docker Hub — primary)
section.para: The published image at docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw is the recommended runtime for most deployments. The image is published in lockstep with the GHCR mirror on every v* git tag; both registries receive identical multi-architecture manifests covering linux/amd64 and linux/arm64. The runtime base is pinned to ubuntu:24.04 (LTS, Noble); the process runs as non-root user opcgw (UID 10001) and exposes OPC UA on port 4855.
Tags follow Semantic Versioning. There is no :latest tag — pin to a specific minor or patch.

row.entry: Exact release. Pin for fully reproducible deployments.
Latest patch within a minor line. Auto-updates on patch releases.
Commit-sha-pinned build for tracing back to a specific commit.

step.para: Install Docker Engine. See docker.com for platform-specific instructions.
Pull the image:

Prepare the host-side bind-mount directories. The container runs as UID 10001 and cannot chown host files, so the operator must set ownership before first start. pki/private additionally requires the NFR9 permission shape (mode 0o700 on the directory, 0o600 on each *.pem file):
step.screen: mkdir -p ./config ./pki/own ./pki/private ./pki/trusted ./pki/rejected ./log ./data chown -R 10001:10001 ./config ./pki ./log ./data chmod 700 ./pki/private find ./pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} + # no-op on fresh install

step.para: Author ./config/config.toml (see — at minimum the [chirpstack] and [opcua] blocks; the [[application]] blocks are optional at this step — the gateway accepts an empty topology and you can add applications, devices, and metrics later via the web UI). For the very first run, the bundled config/config.toml template (with placeholder secrets overridden by env vars) is sufficient even without any application configured.
Start the container, injecting the required secrets via environment variables:
Verify the container is running as a non-root user (UID 10001):
step.screen: docker exec opcgw id # Expected: uid=10001(opcgw) gid=10001(opcgw) groups=10001(opcgw)

note.title: Container Volume Layout
note.para: The container's WORKDIR is /usr/local/bin. The standard bind-mount layout:

listitem.para: /usr/local/bin/config — config.toml, optional log4rs.yaml. Required.
/usr/local/bin/pki — OPC UA PKI directory tree (own/, private/, trusted/, rejected/). Required unless OPC UA security is set to None for both modes and policies.

/usr/local/bin/data — SQLite database file (opcgw.db) plus its WAL + SHM sidecars. Required — without this bind mount the database lives in the ephemeral container layer and every docker rm destroys persisted metrics and the 7-day history table.
/usr/local/bin/log — daily-rolling log files (set OPCGW_LOG_DIR=/usr/local/bin/log or [logging].dir if you prefer a different location).

warning.title: Secret Handling
warning.para: Never bake real API tokens or OPC UA passwords into the Docker image, the config.toml committed to version control, or the docker run command-line in shell history. Inject secrets via -e ENV_VAR=... sourced from a secret manager, via a per-environment .env file consumed by Docker Compose, or via Kubernetes Secrets. The shipped config/config.toml contains placeholder values; the gateway refuses to start with the placeholders in place. See and docs/security.md for the full env-var recipe.

section.title: Docker (GHCR mirror)
section.para: The GitHub Container Registry mirror at ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw is identical to the Docker Hub image (same workflow run, same multi-arch manifest). Use the GHCR mirror when:

listitem.para: Your CI / deployment pipeline is already aligned with the GitHub ecosystem (docker/login-action against github.actor + secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN, etc.).
You are hitting Docker Hub's anonymous-pull rate limit on a deployment fleet and prefer GHCR's higher anonymous allowance.

section.para: Otherwise the procedure is identical to ; only the docker pull and docker run image references change:
section.screen:
docker pull ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw:2.0
docker run -d \ --name opcgw \ --restart unless-stopped \ -p 4855:4855 \ -e OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN='<your-chirpstack-api-token>' \ -e OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD='<your-opc-ua-user-password>' \ -v "$(pwd)/config:/usr/local/bin/config" \ -v "$(pwd)/pki:/usr/local/bin/pki" \ -v "$(pwd)/log:/usr/local/bin/log" \ -v "$(pwd)/data:/usr/local/bin/data" \ ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw:2.0

section.title: Docker Compose
section.para: For convenience, the repository ships a docker-compose.yml that wires the container with the canonical bind-mount layout and a .env-driven secret-injection pattern.

step.para: Clone the repository (the Compose recipe and the .env.example template ship in-tree):
Copy .env.example to .env and replace the placeholder values with real secrets:
The required entries are:
Prepare the host-side bind-mount directories with UID 10001 ownership and the NFR9 PKI permission shape — same as the Docker Hub path:
step.screen: mkdir -p ./config ./pki/own ./pki/private ./pki/trusted ./pki/rejected ./log ./data chown -R 10001:10001 ./config ./pki ./log ./data chmod 700 ./pki/private find ./pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} + # no-op on fresh install

step.para: Start the gateway in detached mode:

Compose's :?err env-var guard pattern in docker-compose.yml causes docker compose up to fail fast with a clear error message if either required variable is unset or empty — no silent empty-string passthrough to the gateway. Note that :?err does not detect a literal placeholder string like REPLACE_ME_WITH_…; if you copy .env.example without editing it, Compose will start the container and the gateway's own startup-validation (Story 7-1) catches the placeholder one layer later with a clear error in the logs.
Tail the startup logs to watch the first ChirpStack poll cycle:

note.title: Choosing the Image
note.para: The shipped docker-compose.yml pins image: docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw:2.0, which pulls the published Docker Hub image. To switch to the GHCR mirror (e.g. in environments that block Docker Hub or to avoid the public-rate-limit on anonymous pulls), edit the image: line to ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw:2.0. To run a locally-built image instead (development, fork builds), run docker build -t opcgw . in the project root and change the image: line to opcgw.

section.title: systemd Service (native binary)
section.para: For deployments without a container runtime, install a pre-built binary (or build from source — see ) and supervise it via systemd. This path is well-suited to industrial-edge appliances and to the from-source release flow.

step.para: Build or download the release binary and copy it into /usr/local/bin/opcgw:
Create a dedicated unprivileged service user and a state directory:
step.screen: sudo useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /usr/sbin/nologin opcgw sudo install -d -m 0755 -o opcgw -g opcgw /var/lib/opcgw sudo install -d -m 0755 -o opcgw -g opcgw /var/lib/opcgw/pki/own sudo install -d -m 0700 -o opcgw -g opcgw /var/lib/opcgw/pki/private sudo install -d -m 0755 -o opcgw -g opcgw /var/lib/opcgw/pki/trusted sudo install -d -m 0755 -o opcgw -g opcgw /var/lib/opcgw/pki/rejected sudo install -d -m 0755 -o opcgw -g opcgw /etc/opcgw sudo install -d -m 0755 -o opcgw -g opcgw /var/log/opcgw

step.para: Place config.toml at /etc/opcgw/config.toml (mode 0o644) and the secret env-file at /etc/opcgw/env (mode 0o600, owned by the service user). The env-file mirrors the .env file used by Docker Compose:

warning.title: systemd EnvironmentFile syntax
warning.para: systemd does not interpret shell quotes or escapes in EnvironmentFile=. Avoid =, leading or trailing whitespace, and embedded newlines in values. Tokens containing $ are passed through literally (no variable expansion); single-quoted or double-quoted values are not stripped of their quote characters — the quotes become part of the value.

step.para: If you intend to enable the embedded Web UI ([web].enabled = true), also deploy the static/ directory under WorkingDirectory so Axum's ServeDir can dispatch the pages — the gateway resolves static/ relative to its CWD:
step.screen: sudo install -d -m 0755 -o opcgw -g opcgw /var/lib/opcgw/static sudo find static -maxdepth 1 -type f \( -name '*.html' -o -name '*.js' -o -name '*.css' \) \ -exec install -m 0644 -o opcgw -g opcgw -t /var/lib/opcgw/static/ {} +
step.para: Skip this step for OPC-UA-only deployments — the static payload is only needed when the web UI is enabled.
Write the unit file at /etc/systemd/system/opcgw.service:
step.programlisting:
[Unit] Description=opcgw — ChirpStack to OPC UA Gateway After=network-online.target Wants=network-online.target
[Service] Type=simple User=opcgw Group=opcgw WorkingDirectory=/var/lib/opcgw EnvironmentFile=/etc/opcgw/env ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/opcgw -c /etc/opcgw/config.toml ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID Restart=on-failure RestartSec=5 # Sandboxing — NFR9 alignment ProtectSystem=strict ProtectHome=true NoNewPrivileges=true PrivateTmp=true ReadWritePaths=/var/lib/opcgw /var/log/opcgw
[Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target
step.para: The ExecReload line wires systemctl reload opcgw to the configuration hot-reload SIGHUP handler (Story 9-7 — see ).
Enable and start the service:
step.screen: sudo systemctl daemon-reload sudo systemctl enable --now opcgw sudo systemctl status opcgw

step.para: Tail the journal to watch startup:

section.title: Build from Source
section.para: A from-source build is appropriate when you need platform-specific optimizations, debug symbols, a custom Rust toolchain, or are working on a development checkout.

step.para: Install build prerequisites (Debian / Ubuntu):
step.screen: sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y protobuf-compiler libssl-dev pkg-config build-essential
step.para: Equivalent packages are available on RHEL / Fedora (protobuf-compiler openssl-devel pkgconfig), Alpine (protoc openssl-dev pkgconfig), and macOS via Homebrew (brew install protobuf pkg-config).
Install the Rust toolchain (1.94.0 or later):
Clone the repository:
For a development build, run a release compile:
For a production install, use cargo install which places the binary at ~/.cargo/bin/opcgw (or whatever CARGO_INSTALL_ROOT points at):
Then wire it into systemd per .

note.title: Build Time
note.para: Release builds take roughly 5-10 minutes on a first compile (the protocol-buffer code is generated at build time via tonic-build). Subsequent incremental builds are measured in seconds.

section.title: Post-install Verification
section.para: The verification steps below are deployment-agnostic — they work for Docker, Compose, systemd, or a foreground binary. Adjust the log-tail recipe to match your deployment shape.

step.para: Confirm the OPC UA endpoint is bound. From the host running the gateway:
step.screen: nc -z localhost 4855 && echo "OPC UA endpoint reachable"

step.para: Confirm the gateway has logged its startup checkpoints. Pick the recipe matching your deployment shape:
step.screen:
# Docker docker logs opcgw 2>&1 | grep -E '(logging_init|chirpstack_connect|opcua_limits_configured)'
# Docker Compose docker compose logs opcgw 2>&1 | grep -E '(logging_init|chirpstack_connect|opcua_limits_configured)'
# systemd journalctl -u opcgw -n 200 | grep -E '(logging_init|chirpstack_connect|opcua_limits_configured)'
# Foreground binary writing to ./log/opc_ua_gw.log tail -n 200 log/opc_ua_gw.log | grep -E '(logging_init|chirpstack_connect|opcua_limits_configured)'

step.para: Confirm the first ChirpStack poll cycle has completed (typically within 30 s of startup, depending on [chirpstack].polling_frequency):
step.screen: # Docker — same shape; Compose / systemd / file-tail variants per above. docker logs opcgw 2>&1 | grep 'poll_cycle_end'
step.para: If the gateway has not produced operation="poll_cycle_end" within ~30 s, head straight to .
(Docker only) Verify the container is running as a non-root user — never as root:
step.screen: docker exec opcgw id # Expected: uid=10001(opcgw) gid=10001(opcgw) groups=10001(opcgw)

step.para: (Optional — Web UI enabled) Verify the embedded web server is reachable and refusing unauthenticated access:
step.screen: curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://localhost:8080/ # Expected: 401 (with WWW-Authenticate: Basic realm="opcgw") # Substitute the configured user_name (default "opcua-user") + your password: curl -sS -u "$OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_NAME:$OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD" \ http://localhost:8080/api/health # Expected: {"status":"ok"}

important.para: The embedded Web UI is HTTP-only — Basic-auth credentials transit in cleartext. For any deployment beyond a trusted LAN, deploy a TLS-terminating reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Traefik) in front of the gateway and bind the listener to loopback. See for the full hardening recipe.

section.title: Configuration Overview
section.para: opcgw is configured through a single TOML file (typically config/config.toml). Every value can be overridden by an environment variable. The location of the configuration file is resolved in this order:

listitem.para: The -c PATH CLI flag.
The CONFIG_PATH environment variable.

Default: config/config.toml (the config directory under the binary's working directory). Only the CONFIG_PATH environment variable above and the -c PATH CLI flag override this default; there is no OPCGW_CONFIG_PATH environment variable.

section.para: Environment variable overrides use the OPCGW_ prefix. Nested keys use __ (double underscore) as the separator. For example, [chirpstack].polling_frequency can be overridden by OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__POLLING_FREQUENCY=30. Two convenience aliases bypass the nesting convention:

listitem.para: OPCGW_LOG_DIR — quick override for [logging].dir, read directly in main.rs before tracing initialises.
OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL — quick override for [logging].level, read directly in main.rs.

section.title: First-run setup wizard (Epic C C-0)
section.para: If you start opcgw with no OPC UA password configured through any source (no [opcua].user_password in config/config.toml, no OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD environment variable, and no config/secrets.toml sibling file), the gateway enters first-run mode:

listitem.para: The OPC UA server binds on its configured endpoint but rejects every authentication attempt (OpcgwAuthManager::is_configured = false). External uptime probes can confirm the gateway is alive, but no SCADA client can connect until setup is complete.

The web UI at http://<host>:<web-port>/ redirects every non-wizard, non-static request to /setup, where a one-shot password-setup form is served. The wizard is the only reachable page until completion.

section.para: Walk-through:

listitem.para: Operator opens the web UI in a browser. Any URL (including /) redirects to /setup.
Operator enters a password and a confirmation, then submits. The server validates: non-empty, no leading or trailing whitespace, not a placeholder string, and the two fields match.

On success, the password is persisted to config/secrets.toml with file mode 0600 (owner read+write only). The file is gitignored. The gateway then signals a graceful shutdown by cancelling the internal CancellationToken.

The supervisor (Docker restart policy / systemctl Restart=on-failure) restarts opcgw automatically. On the second boot, the figment provider stack picks up secrets.toml between the main config.toml and the env-var layer. AppConfig::is_first_run() now returns false, basic auth comes online with the new password, and the OPC UA server accepts authenticated connections.

section.para: One-shot semantics. Post-first-run, /setup returns HTTP 410 Gone. To rotate the password later: either override via OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD, or hand-edit config/secrets.toml and restart.

Trust model. The wizard is intended to be reached on a trusted operator network. For public-internet deployments, set OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD via env-var before the first start to skip the wizard entirely. See docs/security.md § "First-run wizard" for the full threat model.

Wizard scope: password only. The wizard collects ONLY the OPC UA user_password. Every other configuration field (ChirpStack credentials, OPC UA endpoint + PKI directory + application name, web admin credentials, etc.) must already be present in config/config.toml (or provided via OPCGW_* environment variables) before the gateway starts. A truly-empty config.toml will fail validation BEFORE the wizard becomes reachable. As of iter-2 (P23), the [opcua].user_name field defaults to "opcua-user" via a serde default, so a minimal bootstrap config can omit it. The shipped config/config.toml in the repository is the recommended starting point: copy it, fill in your ChirpStack and admin credentials, and leave user_password unset.

Empty [[application]] tolerance. The first-run wizard is paired with empty-application-list tolerance — the validator accepts an application_list = [] baseline, so a fresh container with no configured applications can boot, present the wizard, and then be configured entirely through the web UI (in conjunction with Epic C's downstream stories C-1 through C-6).

section.title: Adding applications, devices, and metrics via the web UI (Epic C C-2)
section.para: From Epic C C-2 onward, the /applications.html and /devices-config.html pages offer name-driven pickers instead of the legacy free-form UUID/DevEUI text inputs. The pickers are populated from GET /api/inventory/{applications,devices,uplinks} (Story C-1) — the operator never needs to copy a UUID from ChirpStack into opcgw by hand.
The full add-flow is application → device → metric:

listitem.para: On /applications.html, the Create application form shows a dropdown sourced from /api/inventory/applications. Pick an application by name; the hidden application_id pre-fills and the Application Name field defaults to the ChirpStack name (operator-editable). Submit lands the application in config/config.toml through the existing CRUD path.

On /devices-config.html, each already-configured application has an Add device sub-form. The dropdown fetches /api/inventory/devices?application_id=<id> and lets the operator pick a device by name. The DevEUI is shown beneath the picker as a small read-only footnote so the operator can copy it for ChirpStack-side troubleshooting if needed.

The metric-picker sub-form is fed by /api/inventory/uplinks?dev_eui=<eui>&limit=10 and renders the observed_keys aggregate as a multi-select checkbox list. Each row carries an auto-inferred wire-type (Float | Int | Bool | String) which the operator can override per row. Tick any combination of keys to add them as metrics in one submission.

section.para: Manual-entry fallback (load-bearing). Every picker carries a Switch to manual entry toggle. The toggle also fires automatically when the inventory endpoint returns HTTP 502 (ChirpStack unreachable) so the operator never gets trapped. The chosen mode persists per page via localStorage (opcgw.picker.applications.mode, opcgw.picker.devices.mode, opcgw.picker.metrics.mode) so an operator who prefers manual entry permanently doesn't have to re-toggle on every page load.
Three failure modes are covered:

listitem.para: ChirpStack maintenance window — picker auto-fallbacks; operator can still configure opcgw using values they know.
Device not yet enrolled in ChirpStack — picker shows an empty-state and flips to manual; the operator can pre-create opcgw config so the device works the moment its first uplink lands.

Metric not yet emitted by the codec — the uplink picker shows no recent uplinks and falls back to manual metric-row entry so the operator can hand-type a metric_name they know the codec will eventually produce.

section.para: Re-fetch on demand. Each picker has a small ↻ refresh icon that calls the corresponding /api/inventory/* endpoint with ?refresh=true, bypassing the server-side TTL cache (default 60 seconds). Useful when an operator added a device to ChirpStack in another browser tab and wants to see it in opcgw immediately.
Audit signals. The picker emits four audit events into the canonical structured log:

listitem.para: event="picker_opened" — operator opened a picker; carries picker_resource and the relevant cache_status.

event="picker_manual_fallback" — picker switched to manual entry; carries picker_resource and a reason (operator_choice, chirpstack_unreachable, chirpstack_error, chirpstack_empty, or no_recent_uplinks).

event="picker_audit_rejected" — POST /api/audit/picker-event rejected an unknown event name or a cross-origin CSRF attempt; carries reason (unknown_event or csrf) and the rejected event name (cap at 256 bytes).

event="metric_wire_type_inferred" — per-metric audit emitted server-side from the POST|PUT /api/applications/<app>/devices[/<dev>] success path when the metric request carries a picker_metadata envelope. Records the inferred type, the operator's final choice, and the sample-values-count from the uplink stream.

section.para: See docs/inventory-api.md § Picker-event audit endpoint for the full request/response contract.

section.title: Reconciling drift between opcgw and ChirpStack (Epic C C-4)
section.para: From Epic C C-4 onward, the /inventory-drift.html page compares opcgw's configured applications, devices, and metrics against ChirpStack's current inventory and highlights stale, missing, available, and renamed resources. This closes the long-tail operational loop that the pickers (C-2) and duplicate prevention (C-3) do not address — what happens when the ChirpStack-side inventory changes after opcgw has been configured.
Every resource is classified into one of four buckets:

listitem.para: ok — present in both opcgw and ChirpStack, names match. Information only; no action needed.

stale — configured in opcgw, no longer in ChirpStack (or, for metrics, not seen in the last 10 uplinks). Operator can Remove from opcgw or Keep as alias. Soft-stale metrics (codec emits the key conditionally) are rendered in a softer yellow tone vs hard-stale red.
available — present in ChirpStack, not in opcgw. Add to opcgw deep-links to the C-2 picker page with the chosen resource pre-selected.
drifted — present in both but names (applications/devices) or wire types (metrics) do not match. Update opcgw name to ChirpStack's or Keep opcgw alias.

note.title: No background polling
note.para: The drift view is operator-triggered only — opcgw does not periodically compute drift. Every page load (and Refresh now click) forces a fresh fetch from ChirpStack (?refresh=true), bypassing the C-1 inventory cache. This bounds the ChirpStack-side load to active reconciliation sessions.

section.para: Typical scenarios:

listitem.para: The device shows as stale. Remove from opcgw deletes the device + its metric mappings (cascade scope shown in the confirmation modal). ChirpStack-side resources are untouched.
The device shows as available. Add to opcgw deep-links to /devices-config.html with the device picker pre-selected; the operator picks metrics from the recent-uplinks list and saves.

The application shows as drifted with both names visible. Update opcgw name to ChirpStack's issues a PUT /api/applications/<id> with the new name; Keep opcgw alias emits a drift_dismissed audit event so the deliberate choice is traceable.

varlistentry.term: Codec firmware adds a metric key

listitem.para: The metric shows as available. Add to opcgw deep-links to the metric picker with the key pre-ticked.

varlistentry.term: Configured wire-type doesn't match observed uplinks

listitem.para: The metric shows as drifted with drift_details.reason="wire_type_mismatch". The drift view links to the devices-config page so the operator can edit the metric type via the canonical edit flow.

section.para: When ChirpStack is unreachable, the page renders the opcgw-side rows as ok placeholders, surfaces a banner explaining the situation, and disables all destructive action buttons + hides Add to opcgw buttons. A Retry button next to the banner re-fires the drift fetch.
Audit events fired by the drift view:

listitem.para: drift_view_opened on every GET /api/inventory/drift call, carrying the summary counts.
drift_action when the operator clicks an action button (carries the action verb, resource scope, and optional operator_choice text).
drift_dismissed when the operator clicks Keep as alias or Keep opcgw alias — the deliberate "known divergence" signal.

section.para: See docs/web-api.md § Story C-4 for the full endpoint contract.

section.title: [global] — Global Settings
section.para: The [global] section currently carries a single flag:
section.programlisting: [global] # Verbose internal debug output (currently a no-op operationally; # reserved for future debug-only behaviours that should not depend on # the tracing log level). debug = true
section.para: All log-related configuration lives under the dedicated [logging] section described next.

section.title: [logging] — Logging
section.para: The [logging] section configures the file log directory and the global verbosity level. Both keys are optional; sensible defaults apply when omitted.
section.programlisting:
[logging] # Directory where per-module daily-rolling log files are written. # Default: "./log" dir = "/var/log/opcgw"
# Global default log level: trace, debug, info, warn, error. # Default: "info" level = "info"

listitem.para: Directory containing the five daily-rolling log files (opc_ua_gw.log, chirpstack.log, opc_ua.log, storage.log, config.log). The directory is created with parents at startup if it does not already exist; if the configured directory is not writable, opcgw falls back to ./log and emits a stderr warning.

Default verbosity for the console (stderr) and the root opc_ua_gw.log. Per-module log files capture at TRACE independently of this value, so even at level = "error" you still get full per-module traces in chirpstack.log and friends.

section.para: Verbosity precedence (highest first):

listitem.para: CLI -d count (-d = debug, -dd = trace, -ddd = trace, etc.).
OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL environment variable.
OPCGW_LOGGING__LEVEL figment-style override.
[logging].level in the TOML file.
Hard-coded default: info.

section.para: Level semantics:

table.title: Log levels and their use cases

row.entry: Deepest debugging — every operation, every span entry
Production troubleshooting — key decisions, timings, IDs
Normal operations — cycle starts/ends, state transitions
Anomalies and retries that succeeded — early warning
Unrecoverable conditions only — silent if healthy

section.para: See for the operations reference, diagnostic patterns, and the symptom cookbook.

section.title: [chirpstack] — ChirpStack Connection
section.programlisting:
[chirpstack] # ChirpStack server address (gRPC endpoint, http:// or https://) server_address = "http://chirpstack.example.com:8080"
# API token issued by ChirpStack. NEVER commit a real token to VCS; # use OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN at deploy time. api_token = "your_api_token_here"
# Tenant UUID tenant_id = "ffffffff-ffff-ffff-ffff-ffffffffffff"
# Polling frequency in seconds (default: 10) polling_frequency = 10
# Connection retries when ChirpStack is unreachable (default: 10) retry = 10
# Delay (seconds) between two retries (default: 10) delay = 10
# Page size for pagination in list APIs (default: 100, range 1-1000) list_page_size = 100

warning.title: API Token Security
warning.para: The api_token grants full ChirpStack access. Do not commit production tokens. Override at deploy time:
The token is never written to log output (a security regression test in the suite asserts this).

section.title: [opcua] — OPC UA Server
section.programlisting:
[opcua] # Application identity (mandatory) application_name = "Chirpstack OPC UA Gateway" application_uri = "urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway" product_uri = "urn:chirpstack:opcua:gateway"
# Diagnostics output from the OPC UA stack itself. # REQUIRED true when [opcua].max_connections is set — the session-count # gauge and the at-limit warn both read async-opcua's CurrentSessionCount # diagnostics variable. Startup fail-fasts otherwise. diagnostics_enabled = true
# Optional: TCP hello timeout (default 5 s) # hello_timeout = 10
# Optional: bind address (default: first interface IP) # host_ip_address = "0.0.0.0"
# Optional: TCP port (default 4855 for this project) # host_port = 4855
# PKI configuration (relative paths are resolved under pki_dir). # create_sample_keypair = false is the production default (Story 7-2). # Set to true ONLY for development self-signed keypair auto-generation. create_sample_keypair = false certificate_path = "own/cert.der" private_key_path = "private/private.pem" trust_client_cert = true check_cert_time = true pki_dir = "./pki"
# User authentication (single user — multi-user RBAC is out of scope). # The password placeholder in the shipped TOML is rejected at startup — # always inject via OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD env var. user_name = "opcua-user" user_password = "REPLACE_ME_WITH_OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD_ENV_VAR"
# Staleness threshold in seconds (Story 5-2). # Metrics older than this return Uncertain in OPC UA; # older than 24 h return Bad. Default: 120 (2 x default polling_frequency). # Range: 1-86400. Override: OPCGW_OPCUA__STALE_THRESHOLD_SECONDS stale_threshold_seconds = 120
# Maximum concurrent OPC UA sessions (Story 7-3). New sessions beyond # this limit are rejected with BadTooManySessions. Existing sessions # unaffected. Default: 10. Range: 1-4096. Setting 0 or > 4096 fails # startup. Override: OPCGW_OPCUA__MAX_CONNECTIONS # max_connections = 10
# Subscription / message-size limits (Story 8-2). Optional; default to # async-opcua library defaults. Override: OPCGW_OPCUA__<UPPER> # max_subscriptions_per_session = 10 # range 1-1000 # max_monitored_items_per_sub = 1000 # range 1-100000 # max_message_size = 327675 # range 1-268435456 (≈ 256 MiB) # max_chunk_count = 5 # range 1-4096
# HistoryRead per-call response cap (Story 8-3, FR22). Caps the number # of HistoryData rows returned per NodeId per HistoryRead call. SCADA # clients page via follow-up calls (manual paging). Default 10000 ≈ # 28 h of poll data at 10 s polling. Range: 1-1000000. # max_history_data_results_per_node = 10000
section.para: The gateway advertises three OPC UA endpoints on the same TCP port. SCADA clients pick the endpoint matching their security posture; the gateway accepts the user-token credentials against any of the three.

row.entry: Development / first-run smoke tests on trusted LANs.
Signed, unencrypted traffic when LAN traffic must remain inspectable.
Production default. Highest security level the gateway advertises.

section.para: The PKI directory layout (pki/own/, pki/private/, pki/trusted/, pki/rejected/) is documented in docs/security.md § "OPC UA security endpoints and authentication".

listitem.para: Drives the OPC UA StatusCode returned with each metric read. See .

When true, opcgw auto-generates a self-signed certificate on first start. Production default is false — provide real CA-signed (or self-signed) certificates manually at pki/own/cert.der and pki/private/private.pem. Release builds with create_sample_keypair = true emit a startup warning.

Caps concurrent OPC UA sessions (Story 7-3). New sessions beyond the cap are rejected with BadTooManySessions; existing sessions unaffected. A misbehaving SCADA reconnect loop or a deliberate session flood cannot exhaust file descriptors or memory. Sized per (N SCADA clients × sessions each) + 20% headroom; values above 50 should prompt a deployment review. Hard cap 4096 (fd-exhaustion guard). Inspect utilisation via event="opcua_session_count" info gauge and at-limit rejections via event="opcua_session_count_at_limit" warns.

Story 8-2 subscription / message-size shaping knobs fed into async-opcua's ServerBuilder at startup. Defaults match async-opcua 0.17.1 library defaults; uncomment only if a deployment scenario requires tuning. Resolved values are echoed at startup as event="opcua_limits_configured".

Per-NodeId HistoryRead response cap (Story 8-3, FR22). SCADA clients exceeding the cap page via follow-up calls (manual paging — continuation points are not yet implemented; see docs/security.md § "Historical data access").

warning.title: OPC UA Private-Key Permissions (NFR9)
warning.para: Story 7-2 enforces filesystem permissions on the OPC UA private key at startup, fail-closed. The gateway refuses to start if any pki/private/*.pem file is not at mode 0o600, or if the parent pki/private directory is not at mode 0o700. The error message includes the observed mode and the chmod recipe. Operator remediation:
warning.screen: chmod 700 pki/private find ./pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} + # no-op on fresh install
warning.para: Silently running with a world-readable private key is worse than refusing to start — this fail-closed behaviour is intentional.

warning.title: OPC UA Authentication Audit Trail
warning.para: Every failed OPC UA authentication emits a structured warn! event (event="opcua_auth_failed") in log/opc_ua.log. The submitted username is sanitised (control chars escaped, truncated to 64 chars); the attempted password is never logged. Source IP is correlated from async-opcua's info!-level "Accept new connection from ..." line by timestamp (NFR12 two-event pattern) — keep OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL at info or below to preserve the correlation, or rely on the per-module opc_ua.log file appender (captures at DEBUG independently).

section.title: [web] — Embedded Web UI
section.para: Story 9-1 ships an embedded Axum HTTP server gated by HTTP Basic auth. Off by default — existing operators upgrading from Phase A see no behavioural change unless they explicitly enable it. The credentials are shared with the [opcua] section (same user_name / user_password); one rotation step covers both surfaces.
section.programlisting:
[web] # Master switch — default false. # Override: OPCGW_WEB__ENABLED=true enabled = true
# Listening port. Default 8080. Range 1024-65535 (privileged ports rejected). port = 8080
# Bind address. Default "0.0.0.0" (all interfaces). Use "127.0.0.1" if a # reverse proxy on the same host fronts the gateway. bind_address = "0.0.0.0"
# HTTP Basic auth realm string. Default "opcgw". Max 64 chars, ASCII, # no '"', no '\', no leading/trailing whitespace. auth_realm = "opcgw"
# Story 9-4 CSRF allow-list for state-changing routes (POST/PUT/DELETE). # Each entry must parse as scheme://host[:port] (no path/query/fragment). # When omitted, defaults to ["http://<bind_address>:<port>"]. # allowed_origins = ["http://gateway.local:8080"]

listitem.para: Live status dashboard (Story 9-2). Single-screen view of ChirpStack connection state, last-poll timestamp, cumulative error count, configured application + device counts. Auto-refreshes every 10 s; mobile-responsive layout; OS-driven dark mode. Underlying JSON shape at GET /api/status.

Live metric values page (Story 9-3, extended by Story A-6). Per-application / per-device grid showing metric name, typed value (Float / Int / Bool / String — post-Epic-A), unit, last-updated timestamp, and a staleness badge (Good / Uncertain / Bad / Missing). JSON shape at GET /api/devices.
Minimal smoke endpoint returning {"status":"ok"}. Used by integration tests and external monitoring probes.

POST / PUT / DELETE on the application, device, and command resources. CSRF-protected via [web].allowed_origins. Audit events: application_* / device_* / command_* + *_crud_rejected on validation failure. State-changing mutations also fire topology_change_detected if the OPC UA address space needs reshaping.

section.para: Configuration hot-reload (Story 9-7) — sending SIGHUP to the gateway PID (systemctl reload opcgw via the ExecReload in the systemd unit, or kill -HUP $(pgrep opcgw)) re-reads config/config.toml through the same figment chain used at startup, validates the candidate, and classifies the diff into three buckets:

listitem.para: Hot-reload-safe — applied without restart at the next safe checkpoint. chirpstack.polling_frequency, chirpstack.retry, chirpstack.delay, chirpstack.list_page_size, the web-side [opcua].stale_threshold_seconds interpretation, and address-space-mutating changes to [[application]] / [[application.device]] / [[application.device.read_metric]] / [[application.device.command]] (Story 9-8).

Restart-required — reload is rejected with event="config_reload_failed" reason="restart_required" and a changed_knob field naming the offending field. Includes chirpstack.server_address, chirpstack.api_token, chirpstack.tenant_id, [opcua].host_ip_address, [opcua].host_port, PKI paths, OPC UA session caps, OPC UA / web credentials, and [web].port / bind_address / enabled / auth_realm.

v1 limitations — credential rotation requires restart in v1 (the auth-middleware refactor to support in-place digest replacement is deferred); [opcua].stale_threshold_seconds hot-reload affects only the web dashboard's Good → Uncertain boundary (OPC UA per-variable read-callback closures capture the threshold at startup). See docs/security.md § "Configuration hot-reload" for the full taxonomy.

warning.title: Web UI is HTTP-only — TLS via reverse proxy
warning.para: Story 9-1 binds an HTTP listener; credentials transit in cleartext. Enable [web] only on trusted LANs. For internet exposure or any path crossing an untrusted hop, deploy a TLS-terminating reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Traefik) in front of the gateway and set bind_address = "127.0.0.1" so the listener is loopback-only.

section.title: [command_delivery] — Command Queue
section.programlisting:
[command_delivery] # Polling interval for ChirpStack delivery confirmations (default 5 s) status_poll_interval_secs = 5
# Maximum time to wait for ChirpStack to confirm a Sent command (default 60 s). # Commands still pending after this become Failed. confirmation_timeout_secs = 60
# Frequency of the timeout-detection scan (default 10 s) timeout_check_interval_secs = 10
# Retention period for completed commands (default 7 days). # Confirmed/Failed commands are purged after this window. history_retention_days = 7

section.title: [[application]] — Devices and Metrics
section.para: The address space exposed via OPC UA mirrors the configured application → device → metric hierarchy. Each application is an array entry under [[application]] and may contain any number of devices and metrics.
section.programlisting:
[[application]] application_id = "ae2012c2-75a1-407d-98ab-1520fb511edf" application_name = "Arrosage"
[[application.device]] device_name = "Niveau_citerne" device_id = "a840418371886840" # DevEUI
[[application.device.read_metric]] metric_name = "Niveau_cit" # OPC UA browse name chirpstack_metric_name = "Water_deep_cm" # ChirpStack metric key metric_type = "Float" # Bool | Int | Float | String metric_unit = "cm" # optional, web-dashboard suffix
[[application.device]] device_name = "Vanne01" device_id = "524d1e0a02243201"
[[application.device.read_metric]] metric_name = "Status_v01" chirpstack_metric_name = "valveStatusCode" metric_type = "Int"
[[application.device.command]] command_name = "Open_close_valve01" command_id = 1 command_confirmed = true # request ChirpStack delivery confirmation command_port = 10 # downlink fPort

listitem.para: application_id must match a real ChirpStack application UUID — opcgw uses it to scope the List calls.
device_id must match the DevEUI of a real device.

[[application.device.read_metric]] declares uplink metrics. As of Epic A (v2.0 GA) the MetricType enum is payload-bearing — values carry real measurements end-to-end. The metric_type string in TOML drives both the persisted variant in SQLite and the OPC UA Variant projection:

table.title: metric_type to OPC UA Variant mapping

row.entry: Variant::Float(f32) — narrowed at the OPC UA boundary; emits event="metric_read" reason="narrowing_overflow" | "narrowing_underflow" on lossy narrowing (Story A-4)
Variant::Int32(i32) when the value fits in i32; Variant::Int64(i64) otherwise (counters, large-magnitude metrics)

listitem.para: metric_unit (Story A-6) is an optional human-readable unit string surfaced on the web dashboard as a value suffix (e.g. 34.2 %, 17.5 cm). Empty-string units coalesce to no-suffix. Not yet exposed via the OPC UA EngineeringUnits property — that is tracked as a future enhancement.

The OPC UA NodeId for each metric is built as format!("{}/{}", device.device_id, metric_name) — the post-issue-#99 fix (docs/security.md § "OPC UA NodeId format (Issue #99 fix, 2026-05-02)") replaced the pre-Epic-7 collision-prone format that conflated different-application metrics sharing the same metric name.

[[application.device.command]] declares a downlink command surface. OPC UA writes against the corresponding node enqueue a downlink with the configured command_id and command_port; when command_confirmed = true the queue tracks delivery state and times out per the [command_delivery] settings.

note.title: Constraints
note.para: The TOML may be empty (no [[application]] blocks at all) — the gateway will start with an empty OPC UA browse tree and the web UI is then used to add applications, devices and metrics interactively. When applications are declared in the TOML, each application must contain at least one device, each device at least one read metric (commands are optional), and metric names must be unique within a device. application_id must be unique across the configuration; device_id must be unique within one application; chirpstack_metric_name must be unique within one device.

note.title: Audit-event Taxonomy
note.para: Configuration mutations via the web CRUD endpoints (Stories 9-4 / 9-5 / 9-6) and OPC UA address-space mutations (Story 9-8) emit a closed-enum audit-event taxonomy: application_created / application_updated / application_deleted / application_crud_rejected; device_created / device_updated / device_deleted / device_crud_rejected; command_created / command_updated / command_deleted / command_crud_rejected; topology_change_detected + address_space_mutation_succeeded / address_space_mutation_failed. See docs/logging.md § "Audit and diagnostic events" and docs/security.md § "Configuration mutations" for the full reference.

section.title: Environment Variable Overrides
section.para: Every TOML key can be overridden via environment variables. The convention:

listitem.para: Standard nested-key override. Examples:
Convenience aliases read directly during the tracing-bootstrap phase, before the full TOML is loaded.
Overrides the path to config.toml when -c is not passed on the command line.

note.title: Why two aliases for log dir/level?
note.para: The [logging] block needs to be readable before the full configuration is parsed (otherwise the file appender cannot start). The convenience aliases are read by a standalone bootstrap step; the standard OPCGW_LOGGING__* overrides are picked up later when the full AppConfig loads. Both forms are supported; the alias wins if both are set.

section.title: Process Identity
section.para: opcgw runs as an unprivileged user regardless of deployment path:

listitem.para: Docker / Compose — the Dockerfile creates a non-root user opcgw (UID 10001) and drops privileges via USER opcgw before ENTRYPOINT. Verify with docker exec opcgw id — the output must show uid=10001(opcgw), never uid=0(root). Bind-mount host directories must be owned by UID 10001 before first start (see ).

systemd — the canonical unit file () sets User=opcgw / Group=opcgw and uses ProtectSystem=strict + NoNewPrivileges=true to constrain the service. Verify with systemctl show opcgw -p User,Group,NoNewPrivileges.

Foreground binary — the gateway runs as the invoking user. For production, prefer the systemd path so the gateway has a dedicated unprivileged service user. Never run opcgw as root; the OPC UA private-key permission enforcement (NFR9) does not protect against a compromised process running with elevated privileges.
Override the configuration file path. Wins over CONFIG_PATH and the default.
Increase verbosity. -d ⇒ debug; -dd or more ⇒ trace. Wins over every other verbosity source.
Print the help text and exit.
Print the version and exit.

section.title: Startup Verification
section.para: A successful start emits a sequence of structured log lines with microsecond UTC timestamps. Look for these checkpoints:

listitem.para: Tracing initialised (logging_init).
Configuration loaded (operation = "config_loaded" at info).
SQLite connection pool ready and migrations applied.
ChirpStack gRPC channel connected (operation = "chirpstack_connect", success = true).
OPC UA server bound to its endpoint and address space built.
First poll cycle completes (operation = "poll_cycle_start" followed by per-device device_polled debug lines).

example.title: Expected Startup Log Output
example.programlisting: 2026-04-27T12:34:56.123456Z INFO opcgw: tracing initialised level=info dir=./log 2026-04-27T12:34:56.234567Z INFO opcgw::config: operation="config_loaded" applications=4 devices=12 metrics=42 2026-04-27T12:34:56.300100Z INFO opcgw::storage::sqlite: schema_version=5 migrations_applied=0 2026-04-27T12:34:56.501020Z INFO opcgw::chirpstack: operation="chirpstack_connect" attempt=1 endpoint=http://... timeout_secs=0 2026-04-27T12:34:56.612030Z INFO opcgw::chirpstack: operation="chirpstack_connect" attempt=1 latency_ms=110 success=true 2026-04-27T12:34:56.700400Z INFO opcgw::opc_ua: OPC UA limits configured event="opcua_limits_configured" max_sessions=10 [...] 2026-04-27T12:34:57.001234Z INFO opcgw::chirpstack: operation="poll_cycle_start" device_count=12

section.title: Graceful Shutdown
section.para: opcgw treats Ctrl-C and SIGTERM equivalently: a single CancellationToken is shared across the poller, OPC UA server, and pruner; on signal it cancels all three and the main task .awaits their drain.

listitem.para: Inbound OPC UA connections are refused; existing sessions are allowed to close in their own time.
The active poll cycle finishes its in-flight batch transaction (txn_commit) so no metrics are lost.
SQLite connections are returned to the pool and dropped; the WAL is checkpointed.
tracing's non-blocking appenders flush their buffers via their guard objects (held until main() returns).

warning.title: Force Shutdown
warning.para: Avoid SIGKILL (kill -9). The SQLite database is journaled and will recover, but in-flight downlink commands marked Sent may need manual inspection; log lines buffered in the non-blocking appender at the moment of the kill are lost. Always prefer SIGTERM.

section.title: What to Monitor
section.para: The gateway exposes its own health through three channels. For a typical production deployment:

listitem.para: Live health (OPC UA) — read the Gateway folder via OPC UA from your SCADA front-end. See .

Live health (Web UI dashboard) — if [web].enabled = true, browse to http://<gateway-host>:<web-port>/ for a single-screen view of ChirpStack connection state, last-poll timestamp, error count, and configured application / device counts. The metrics page at /metrics.html shows live typed values plus a staleness badge (Good / Uncertain / Bad / Missing) per metric. Both pages auto-refresh every 10 s and expose JSON shapes at /api/status and /api/devices for curl | jq / Prometheus textfile exporters.
Diagnostics — tail the per-module log files or aggregate them through your log shipper. See .

section.title: The Gateway Folder in OPC UA
section.para: Under the configured root, opcgw publishes a synthetic Gateway folder containing three health variables:

listitem.para: Time of the last successful poll cycle. The value is NULL (OPC UA Variant::Empty) until the first cycle completes; reading it before that emits a warn log line with warning="no_data_yet".

Cumulative per-cycle error count carried in the persisted gateway status row. Saturates at i32::MAX; a saturation read emits a warning. Spikes between consecutive cycles (delta >= 5) emit operation = "error_spike".
true when the most recent poll cycle succeeded for at least one device, false otherwise. The first per-cycle ChirpStack failure also emits operation = "chirpstack_outage".

section.para: All three reads are subject to the same staleness logic as regular metrics; under normal operation they return Good.

section.title: Stale-Data Status Codes
section.para: Every metric read returns a value plus an OPC UA StatusCode derived from the metric's age relative to [opcua].stale_threshold_seconds:

row.entry: Last-known value still served, but stale
Decisively stale; treat the value as suspect

section.para: Transitions between status codes emit operation = "staleness_transition": in-flight transitions at info, the cold-start first observation at debug (so restarts of a large stale fleet do not flood the alert pipeline). Metrics whose age sits within ±5 s of the threshold also emit operation = "staleness_boundary" at debug for diagnosability.

listitem.para: Time to complete a full metric poll cycle. Should remain below polling_frequency. Watch the per-cycle duration in the operation = "poll_cycle_end" log line and the budget warning if operation = "batch_write" exceeds 500 ms.
Devices producing successful reads in the most recent cycle. The device_polled success=true count tells you the live fleet; a sudden drop is a signal to investigate ChirpStack or radio coverage.
SQLite database file size growth. The pruner enforces the historical retention window — if the database is growing faster than expected, check the operation = "prune_metric_history" line.

Per-read budget is 100 ms. The gateway emits a warn with exceeded_budget = true on any read past that bound; the Story 6-1 microbench measured p95 around 11 µs at 1 kHz read rate, so budget exceedance is a strong signal of contention.
Per-query budget is 10 ms. Persistent SQLITE_BUSY warnings indicate write contention or pool sizing problems.

section.title: Command Queue Monitoring
section.para: Downlink commands traverse three states:

listitem.para: Pending — Enqueued by an OPC UA write; not yet sent to ChirpStack.
Sent — ChirpStack accepted the downlink. If command_confirmed = true, the gateway polls for confirmation every status_poll_interval_secs.
Confirmed / Failed — Terminal states. A Sent command that does not transition within confirmation_timeout_secs becomes Failed.

section.para: Use the SQLite database to inspect queue state directly when needed:
section.screen: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db \ "SELECT command_id, status, created_at, sent_at, confirmed_at, error_message FROM commands ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;"

section.title: Architecture
section.para: opcgw uses the tracing crate end-to-end. Every log line is structured (key=value fields plus a free-text message), carries a microsecond UTC timestamp via ChronoUtc, and may carry a request_id if it was emitted inside an OPC UA read span.
Five appenders are wired by default:
All file appenders are daily-rolling. The level filter on per-module files is not configurable — operators always have the full per-module trace available even when the console is at error.

section.title: Request Correlation
section.para: Each OPC UA read is wrapped in an info_span! with a freshly generated request_id (UUID v4). All log lines emitted while that span is active inherit the field — including storage_query records from the SQLite layer and staleness_check records inside the OPC UA callback. To trace one client read end-to-end at debug:
The expected order is opc_ua_read entry → storage_query → staleness_check → opc_ua_read exit.

section.title: Performance Budgets
section.para: Three hot paths emit a structured warn with exceeded_budget = true when their wall-clock latency crosses a fixed threshold. The thresholds are constants in src/utils.rs:
Below budget the same call sites stay at debug, so steady-state operation does not flood the warn channel.

section.title: Operation Names — At a Glance
section.para: Every load-bearing log line carries an operation = "<name>" field. The full reference (including expected fields, level, and remediation tips) lives in docs/logging.md; the table below is a quick lookup of the most common names.

row.entry: poller, gRPC channel and probe paths
OPC UA server, every metric read
OPC UA server, every metric read
OPC UA server, on status code change
OPC UA server, ±5 s of threshold
OPC UA server, Gateway folder reads
OPC UA server, first uninitialised read

section.para: Story 4-4 (auto-recovery from ChirpStack outages) ships recovery_attempt, recovery_complete, recovery_failed — see docs/logging.md for the field schema.

section.title: Audit and Diagnostic Events (event=)
section.para: Stories 7-2 onward use a separate event= field (instead of the older operation=) for security-relevant audit events and one-shot diagnostic events. The event= prefix makes them easy to filter with grep 'event="..."' log/*.log. The full reference (closed-enum reason= taxonomy per event, field schemas, severity, and remediation tips) lives in docs/logging.md § "Audit and diagnostic events (event=)" and docs/security.md; the table below indexes the event names introduced through v2.0 GA.

table.title: Audit and diagnostic events introduced through v2.0 GA

row.entry: Story 8-2 — startup echo of subscription / message-size limits
Story 7-2 retro — log-level below info reduces NFR12 source-IP correlation
Story 9-1 — web UI Basic-auth failure (reason ∈ missing, malformed_scheme, malformed_base64, missing_colon, user_mismatch, password_mismatch)
Story 9-7 — reload rejected (reason ∈ validation, io, restart_required)
Stories 9-4 / 9-5 / 9-6 — CRUD mutations and validation rejections; CSRF dispatched per URL path prefix
Stories 9-7 + 9-8 — reload that adds / removes applications / devices / metrics / commands
Story 9-8 — OPC UA address-space runtime mutation outcome
Epic A Story A-3 — poller-side payload conversion (reason ∈ invalid_bool, non_finite, int_overflow)
Epic A Story A-4 — OPC UA Read narrowing (reason ∈ no_payload, narrowing_overflow, narrowing_underflow)
Epic A Story A-5 — OPC UA HistoryRead per-row conversion (reason ∈ schema_drift, unparseable_timestamp, narrowing_overflow, narrowing_underflow)
Epic A Story A-5 — aggregate skip counts per HistoryRead call
Epic A Story A-6 — web-layer JSON serialization (reason ∈ non_finite, int_precision_lossy)

section.para: The wire response to a client never depends on whether an audit event fired — failure modes that surface as event="..._failed" always return the same status code + headers regardless of which internal reason they recorded. The discrimination exists only in the audit log for forensic purposes.

section.title: Symptom Cookbook
section.para: Each subsection below pairs a symptom an operator can observe with the structured log lines that diagnose it and the corrective action.

section.title: Cannot connect to ChirpStack
section.para: Log signature: operation="chirpstack_connect" with success=false, repeated; or a single operation="chirpstack_outage" on the first per-cycle failure.

listitem.para: Confirm the configured server_address resolves and the gRPC port is reachable: nc -vz chirpstack.example.com 8080.

Validate the API token. A 16/Unauthenticated tonic code would surface as a generic OpcGwError::ChirpStack(...); cross-check by hitting ChirpStack directly with grpcurl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ chirpstack:8080 list.

Inspect last_successful_poll in the chirpstack_outage warn line: a long gap indicates a sustained outage. Story 4-4 will introduce automatic reconnection; until then, the poller continues attempting on its normal cadence.

section.title: Attempted to add a duplicate
section.para: Log signature: event="application_crud_rejected" or event="device_crud_rejected" or event="command_crud_rejected", each carrying reason="conflict" conflict_kind="duplicate". For a hot-reload (operator hand-edited config/config.toml and saved): event="config_reload_rejected" reason="conflict" conflict_kind="duplicate".

Story C-3 rejects same-level duplicates server-side: two applications with the same application_id; two devices with the same device_id under one application; two metrics with the same metric_name or chirpstack_metric_name within one device; two commands with the same command_id or command_name within one device.

listitem.para: Find the rejection in the structured log: grep 'conflict_kind="duplicate"' opcgw.log. The field + value + scope fields name exactly what was rejected and at which level.

For an HTTP rejection (web UI / API), the 409 response body has the same shape: {"error":"duplicate","field":"...","value":"...","scope":"...","hint":"..."}. The UI surfaces the hint inline near the offending input — pick a different identifier, or DELETE the existing entry first.

For a config_reload_rejected (TOML hand-edit), the in-memory configuration is unchanged (no partial apply); the on-disk config.toml is also untouched (the gateway does not rewrite operator files). Hand-edit the TOML to fix the duplicate, save the file, and re-trigger the reload (SIGHUP).

Two patterns are explicitly NOT duplicates and remain allowed: the same DevEUI under different applications (one physical sensor exposed under multiple OPC UA namespaces); the same chirpstack_metric_name on different devices (multiple sensors of the same kind).

section.title: Sudden burst of errors
section.para: Log signature: operation="error_spike" with delta >= 5.

listitem.para: Cross-reference the surrounding device_poll warns to identify the affected devices.
If a single fleet-wide gateway, check ChirpStack itself. If a localised group, check the radio path (rssi, snr in ChirpStack UI).
The spike is informational only — it does not gate further polling.

section.title: SQLite busy / locked warnings
section.para: Log signature: operation="storage_query" error="SQLITE_BUSY" sqlite_error_code=DatabaseBusy (or DatabaseLocked).

listitem.para: The sqlite_error_code field distinguishes DatabaseBusy (waiting on a lock) from DatabaseLocked (cross-connection write contention).

WAL mode and the per-task connection pool make rare SQLITE_BUSY tolerable. Sustained busy warnings under steady load suggest pool undersizing or a long-running write — verify the operation = "batch_write" latencies.
Run integrity check during a maintenance window: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db "PRAGMA integrity_check;".

section.title: Performance budgets exceeded
section.para: Log signature: any of opc_ua_read, storage_query, or batch_write with exceeded_budget=true.

listitem.para: Capture the request_id on an opc_ua_read warn and grep the file logs for the full read-path trace.
If the bottleneck is storage_query, check query_type and the SQLITE_BUSY rate at the same time.
If batch_write is slow, the cumulative per-cycle metric count (metrics_count) plus disk I/O contention are the usual culprits.

section.title: OPC UA client sees NULL LastPollTimestamp
section.para: Log signature: operation="health_metric_read" metric="LastPollTimestamp" warning="no_data_yet"; once-per-process operation="gateway_status_init".

listitem.para: This is normal during startup, before the first poll cycle has completed. The gateway is healthy.
If it persists past one polling interval, the first cycle is failing — see .

Verify the OPC UA server bound successfully — search the log for event="opcua_limits_configured" (emitted once by src/opc_ua.rs after the OPC UA server applies its session / subscription / message-size limits) or the periodic event="opcua_session_count" gauge.
Confirm the port is listening: ss -tlnp sport = :4855 (or netstat -tlnp | grep 4855).
Confirm the firewall permits inbound connections to the configured port.
If PKI is enabled, confirm the client certificate is under pki_dir/trusted/. Rejected certificates land in pki_dir/rejected/; copy them across explicitly to trust them.

section.title: OPC UA clients see Uncertain or Bad status
section.para: Log signature: operation="staleness_transition" status_code=Uncertain (or Bad); possibly operation="staleness_boundary" for metrics flickering near the threshold.

listitem.para: Map the affected metric to its device and check that the device is producing uplinks (ChirpStack UI).
Verify polling_frequency against stale_threshold_seconds: the latter should be at least 2× the former so transient missed cycles do not flap the status.
For a fleet-wide Uncertain wave that resolves on its own, suspect a brief ChirpStack outage; cross-check chirpstack_outage in the same time window.

section.title: Database integrity errors
section.para: Log signature: OpcGwError::Storage with messages mentioning "disk image malformed".

listitem.para: Stop the gateway gracefully (SIGTERM) so the WAL is checkpointed.
Back up the database before any recovery action: cp -p data/opcgw.db data/opcgw.db.backup.
Run the SQLite integrity check: sqlite3 data/opcgw.db "PRAGMA integrity_check;". If it returns ok, the issue may have been transient; restart the gateway.
If integrity is broken, the gateway will recreate the schema on next start — but historical metrics are lost. Plan backups accordingly.

section.title: ChirpStack authentication failure
section.para: Symptom: The gateway never logs a successful chirpstack_connect info line; OPC UA reads return the discriminant string or BadDataUnavailable; no poll_cycle_end emissions.
section.screen:
# systemd: journalctl -u opcgw | grep -E 'operation="chirpstack_connect".*(Unauthenticated|PermissionDenied|InvalidArgument)'
# Docker: docker logs opcgw 2>&1 | grep -E 'operation="chirpstack_connect".*(Unauthenticated|PermissionDenied|InvalidArgument)'
section.para: Root cause: Most commonly an unset, rotated, or wrong-tenant OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN. The shipped config/config.toml contains a placeholder token; the gateway refuses to start with the placeholder in place. ChirpStack returns gRPC code 16 / Unauthenticated when the bearer token is invalid.

listitem.para: Confirm the env-var is set in the running process:
listitem.screen: # Docker: docker exec opcgw env | grep -c OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN # Expected: 1 # systemd: sudo systemctl show opcgw -p Environment | grep -c OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN # Inspect the loaded env-file: sudo grep OPCGW_CHIRPSTACK__API_TOKEN /etc/opcgw/env

listitem.para: Issue a fresh ChirpStack API token (ChirpStack v4 UI → API Keys) scoped to the configured [chirpstack].tenant_id. Verify independently with grpcurl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" chirpstack:8080 list.
Inject the new token via the env-var (NOT into config.toml) and restart the gateway (systemctl restart opcgw / docker compose up -d).

section.title: OPC UA connection refused / BadTooManySessions
section.para: Symptom: SCADA client cannot establish a session; existing sessions continue working (suggests Story 7-3 session cap), or all clients are refused (suggests auth / endpoint mismatch).

listitem.para: event="opcua_session_count_at_limit" warns — the [opcua].max_connections cap (default 10) is saturated; new CreateSession attempts get BadTooManySessions. Cross-reference the source_ip field to identify a runaway reconnect loop or a port-scan.
event="opcua_auth_failed" warns — user-token credentials don't match [opcua].user_name / [opcua].user_password. The user field is sanitised; the attempted password is never logged.

For at-limit rejections: raise [opcua].max_connections per the tuning checklist in (sized as N SCADA clients × sessions each + 20% headroom), then SIGHUP — note that max_connections is restart-required per the Story 9-7 hot-reload taxonomy, so a full process restart is needed. Investigate the offending source_ip if the cap rejection is unexpected.

For auth failures: rotate OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD; verify the SCADA client is configured with the matching credential; confirm OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL is info or below so the NFR12 source-IP correlation ("Accept new connection from ..." line by async-opcua) survives.

section.title: Polled metrics not appearing in OPC UA Read
section.para: Symptom: SCADA client reads a metric NodeId and receives BadDataUnavailable (post-Epic-A legacy-row symptom) or the discriminant string "Float" / "Int" (pre-Epic-A symptom — should NOT occur on v2.0 GA). Web dashboard shows the Missing badge.
section.screen: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="metric_parse"' # Inspect the row directly: sqlite3 ./opcgw.db \ "SELECT device_id, metric_name, value_type, value_real, value_int, value_bool, value_text FROM metric_values WHERE device_id='<eui>' AND metric_name='<name>';"

listitem.para: value_type='legacy' rows surface as BadDataUnavailable for ONE poll interval after a Path-A migration () until the next poll cycle UPSERTs a typed payload.

event="metric_parse" reason="non_finite" or reason="invalid_bool" or reason="int_overflow" — the poller skipped this metric for this cycle (Epic A Story A-3 NaN/Inf filter at the poller boundary). The metric is skipped, not silently coerced.
If value_type is real / int / bool / text but the corresponding typed column is NULL: schema drift — investigate via PRAGMA quick_check;.

For legacy rows: wait one poll interval; the row will be replaced with a typed payload on the next successful poll. Persistent legacy rows past one interval indicate the poller cannot reach the device — cross-check device_polled success=true.

For reason="non_finite": investigate sensor calibration. For reason="invalid_bool": confirm device firmware emits 0.0 or 1.0. For reason="int_overflow": the metric exceeds i64 range — check device firmware emit precision.

section.title: HistoryRead returns empty / BadDataUnavailable
section.para: Symptom: SCADA client issues an OPC UA HistoryRead over a 7-day window and gets fewer rows than expected, or every row carries StatusCode::BadDataUnavailable.
section.screen: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="metric_history_summary"' | tail -10 journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="metric_history_read"' | tail -10
