section.para: Root cause: metric_history_summary (trace) is the aggregate-skip telemetry per query_metric_history call; it carries schema_drift_skipped and unparseable_timestamp_skipped counters. Pre-Epic-A rows in metric_history surface as DataValue { value: None, status: BadDataUnavailable } per Story A-5 — they are NOT silently dropped from the stream. Per-row narrowing failures fire metric_history_read with reason ∈ {narrowing_overflow, narrowing_underflow}.

listitem.para: For legacy rows (BadDataUnavailable in the response stream): expected during the Epic A migration window. New rows captured after upgrade carry typed payloads.

For per-call cap saturation: check [opcua].max_history_data_results_per_node (default 10000 ≈ 28 h at 10 s polling). For longer windows, SCADA clients must page via follow-up calls (manual paging — continuation points are not implemented; see docs/security.md § "Historical data access").

section.title: Gateway refuses to start — PKI permissions
section.para: Symptom: opcgw exits at startup with an error message mentioning the private-key file mode or the pki/private directory mode.
section.screen: ls -la pki/private/ # Expected: # drwx------ ... pki/private # -rw------- ... pki/private/private.pem
section.para: Root cause: Story 7-2 enforces NFR9 fail-closed at startup. The gateway refuses to start unless each pki/private/*.pem file is mode 0o600 and the parent directory is mode 0o700. The error text includes the observed mode and a chmod recipe.

listitem.para: Tighten the permissions per the error message:
listitem.screen: chmod 700 pki/private find ./pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} + # no-op on fresh install

listitem.para: For Docker / Compose deployments, the host-side directory must be at the same modes — UID 10001 owns the files and the gateway cannot chmod loose modes (it can only validate them). Apply on the host before docker compose up.
For an upgrade from Story 7-1 deployments (which left private.pem at mode 0o644): run find pki/private -type f -name '*.pem' -exec chmod 600 {} \; then restart.

section.title: Web UI returns 401 / 404
section.para: Symptom: Browser displays the gateway URL's Basic-auth prompt repeatedly (401), or after a successful auth shows a 404 on /index.html or /metrics.html.
section.screen: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="web_auth_failed"' | tail -10 # Verify the web server actually started: journalctl -u opcgw | grep 'event="web_server_started"'

listitem.para: For 401: the web_auth_failed warn carries a reason field (missing, malformed_scheme, malformed_base64, missing_colon, user_mismatch, password_mismatch). Web credentials are shared with [opcua].user_name / [opcua].user_password — check the env vars.

For 404: the embedded web server resolves static/ relative to the gateway's CWD. For Docker the Dockerfile copies static/ into /usr/local/bin/static; for systemd the unit file must set WorkingDirectory=/var/lib/opcgw with static/ deployed there.
Confirm env vars are set:
listitem.screen: # Docker docker exec opcgw env | grep -E 'OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_NAME|OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD|OPCGW_WEB_' # systemd sudo grep -E 'OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_NAME|OPCGW_OPCUA__USER_PASSWORD|OPCGW_WEB_' /etc/opcgw/env

listitem.para: Confirm [web].enabled = true (or OPCGW_WEB__ENABLED=true) and that event="web_server_started" appears within ~5 s of startup. Absence of that line means the web server is disabled.
For 404: verify static/index.html, static/metrics.html, and the JavaScript files are present in the CWD-relative static/ directory.

section.title: Log files growing too fast
section.para: Symptom: The [logging].dir directory grows by hundreds of MB per day, eventually saturating local disk.

Root cause: opcgw uses daily-rolling file appenders (one root + four per-module). The per-module files always capture at TRACE independently of the global level — this is deliberate (forensic detail when the console is intentionally quiet). Volume scales with poll cadence and OPC UA read rate; 10 devices at 1 Hz polling under a SCADA client actively subscribing typically produces 50-200 MB/day.

listitem.para: Configure your platform's log-retention tooling (logrotate, journald with SystemMaxUse=, Docker's --log-opt max-size=) — opcgw itself does not delete old daily-rolled files.
Reduce the global console verbosity via OPCGW_LOG_LEVEL=warn (the per-module files still capture at TRACE, preserving forensic detail).
If using a custom log4rs.yaml (legacy from pre-tracing days), inspect its rolling_file + size + roller stanza for the rotation window.

chapter.title: Upgrade and Migration
chapter.para: This chapter covers operator-driven upgrade procedures. The canonical, exhaustively-tested runbook for the v2.0-rc → v2.0 GA transition (Epic A — payload-bearing MetricType; SQLite schema v006 → v008) lives in docs/deployment-guide.md § "Epic A migration". The summary below is operator-facing; defer to the deployment-guide for the per-line architectural rationale, SLA tests, and the v007 / v008 migration internals.

section.title: Pre-upgrade Checklist
section.para: Before stopping the current (v2.0-rc) gateway:

listitem.para: Take a file-level backup of the SQLite database. The backup is your only rollback path — the migration is one-way. Use the explicit three-path form, never a glob, so unrelated backup files (e.g. opcgw.db.bak.YYYY-MM-DD) are not collected:
Identify your current schema version. Run the bundled pre-flight script:

The script wraps PRAGMA user_version with an operator-friendly recommendation, and pre-checks that the file is actually an opcgw database (has the metric_values and metric_history tables) so you don't accidentally point it at an unrelated SQLite file (Firefox places.sqlite, etc.).
Version 6 — pre-Epic-A. Migration applies.
Version 7 — interrupted prior upgrade left the database between v007 and v008. Starting the v2.0 gateway will complete the migration (idempotent — only v008 will be re-applied).
Version 8 — already migrated. No action needed.

Decide Path A vs Path B. Path A preserves pre-Epic-A historical rows (surfacing them as BadDataUnavailable for one poll interval); Path B drops the database file and starts fresh. For most production deployments Path A is the right choice; test / dev gateways and deployments where pre-Epic-A history is uninteresting can use Path B.

section.title: Path A: In-Place Auto-Migration (Default)
section.para: The recommended path for operators who want to preserve historical metric rows captured under the v2.0-rc schema.

listitem.para: Stop the v2.0-rc gateway gracefully:
listitem.screen:
# systemd: sudo systemctl stop opcgw
# Docker Compose: docker compose stop opcgw
# Foreground binary: Ctrl-C or kill -TERM <pid>

listitem.para: Replace the binary or pull the new image:
listitem.screen:
# Docker Hub: docker pull docker.io/gcorbaz/opcgw:2.0
# GHCR mirror: docker pull ghcr.io/guycorbaz/opcgw:2.0
# Source build: cargo install --path .

listitem.para: Start the v2.0 gateway pointing at the same opcgw.db file. The run_migrations() path applies v007 (typed value columns — metadata-only ALTER TABLE) and v008 (exactly-one-non-NULL CHECK constraint, via CREATE TABLE … AS SELECT wrapped in BEGIN/COMMIT) automatically. Pre-Epic-A rows are tagged value_type = 'legacy' with NULL typed columns by the v007 column default; no rows are dropped.
Verify the migrations applied. Pick the recipe matching your deployment shape:
listitem.screen:
# systemd: journalctl -u opcgw -n 200 | grep -E "Applied migration v00(7|8)"
# Docker Compose: docker compose logs opcgw | grep -E "Applied migration v00(7|8)"
# Foreground binary: tail -n 200 log/opc_ua_gw.log | grep -E "Applied migration v00(7|8)"
listitem.para: You should see two info lines:
listitem.programlisting: Applied migration v007_typed_value_columns version=7 Applied migration v008_typed_value_constraints version=8

listitem.para: Wait for the first poll cycle. OPC UA clients will see BadDataUnavailable on legacy rows for the duration of one poll interval. Once the poller UPSERTs each metric with a typed payload, the legacy rows are replaced and OPC UA Reads return the real measurement.

section.title: Path B: Drop-and-Recreate
section.para: The right path for operators who do not need pre-Epic-A history (test deployments, dev gateways, or production gateways where historical rows are uninteresting under the new typed-payload contract).

listitem.para: Stop the v2.0-rc gateway (same recipes as Path A).

Remove the database file and its sidecar files. Use the explicit three-path form, NOT a glob — a glob like opcgw.db* would also remove your opcgw.db.pre-epic-a.bak backup file and any operator-managed backup snapshots in the same directory:
For Docker bind-mounts, run the rm on the host side directory that's bind-mounted into the container.
Replace the binary or pull the new image (same as Path A).
Start the v2.0 gateway. It creates a fresh v008-schema database with zero rows; the next poll cycle populates metric_values with real typed payloads from the start.

section.title: Rollback Contract
section.para: The Epic A migration is one-way. v007 column additions cannot be cleanly dropped without breaking post-Epic-A data integrity contracts (the typed columns become NOT-NULL-able after v008's CHECK lands, and the MetricType enum no longer carries a string representation). v008's exactly-one-non-NULL CHECK is not in v006 and cannot be added to a v006-shaped database without first running v007.
The only rollback path is to restore the pre-upgrade backup file taken in the pre-upgrade checklist, then run the v2.0-rc binary against it:
section.screen: # Stop the v2.0 gateway first. cp /path/to/opcgw.db.pre-epic-a.bak /path/to/opcgw.db # Restore the v2.0-rc binary or revert the Docker image tag. # Start the v2.0-rc gateway.
section.para: There is no in-tree rollback tool. Take the backup BEFORE starting the upgrade — the migration starts the moment you launch the v2.0 binary against a v006 database.

listitem.para: Schema version is 8:
First poll cycle completed — look for operation="poll_cycle_end" in the gateway logs (Path A: this may take up to one [chirpstack].polling_frequency interval; default 10 s).
listitem.screen:
# systemd: journalctl -u opcgw | grep "poll_cycle_end" | head -3
# Docker Compose: docker compose logs opcgw 2>&1 | grep "poll_cycle_end" | head -3

listitem.para: OPC UA Read returns the actual measurement payload — not the discriminant string (Variant::String("Float")), not BadDataUnavailable for any typed row. Connect a SCADA client (UaExpert, FUXA, Ignition) and read a metric variable. Expected DataValue.value per MetricType variant: see the mapping table in .

(Path A only) Legacy rows clear within one poll interval. Re-read the same metric a poll interval later; if it previously returned BadDataUnavailable, it now returns the freshly UPSERTed typed payload.

section.title: SLA Expectation
section.para: For typical residential / small-scale deployments (≤10k rows per table, ≤10 MB total database size), the v007 + v008 migrations complete in under 5 seconds. This SLA is pinned by regression tests (test_v006_to_v008_full_upgrade_path_under_5s and per-migration siblings).

For larger databases (≥100 MB, typically ≥500k historical rows), v008's CREATE TABLE … AS SELECT pattern is the dominant cost and scales roughly linearly with row count. Expect a multi-minute startup delay on the first upgrade run — the gateway appears unresponsive (OPC UA port not yet bound) until the migration completes. The auto-migration is synchronous before the OPC UA server binds its port — operator-visible behaviour is "the gateway takes longer than usual to start the first time after upgrading", which is expected and not a defect.

section.title: Common Gotchas
section.para: The full list with examples lives in docs/deployment-guide.md § "Common gotchas". The six pitfalls operators most commonly hit:

listitem.para: Legacy rows look like "missing data" in the web dashboard and as BadDataUnavailable in OPC UA Reads — for one poll interval only. Wait one cycle.
The metric_history table is migrated the same way as metric_values. Pre-Epic-A rows surface in HistoryRead responses as DataValue { value: None, status: BadDataUnavailable } — NOT silently dropped.

New audit events may fire on first startup. Epic A introduced five event="metric_*" audit lines; if a v006 database happens to contain pre-A-3-filter non-finite rows, the defensive guards emit warns. Operator- actionable but not blocking.
run_migrations() is idempotent. Re-running the v2.0 binary against a v007 or v008 database is a no-op.
v008's CREATE TABLE … AS SELECT is BEGIN/COMMIT-wrapped. A process kill mid-v008 leaves the database in a consistent v007 state; restart the gateway and v008 retries.

Docker bind mounts preserve opcgw.db across container replacement — Path A "just works" with the standard docker compose pull && docker compose up -d workflow because the database file lives on the host. Path B requires rm ./opcgw.db ./opcgw.db-wal ./opcgw.db-shm in the host-side bind-mount directory (avoid the opcgw.db* glob).

chapter.title: Maintenance
chapter.para: For upgrade and migration procedures (v2.0-rc → v2.0 GA, SQLite schema v006 → v008), see .

section.title: Database Maintenance
section.para: SQLite is largely self-maintaining under WAL mode. Two operations are worth scheduling:

step.para: Trigger SQLite's auto-tuner without stopping the gateway:
The pragma analyses indexes that have changed significantly and rebuilds plan statistics — runs in milliseconds on the production-sized database.
Stop opcgw with SIGTERM.
Run:
Expected output: ok.
Restart opcgw.

section.title: Historical Metric Pruning
section.para: The metric_history table is append-only and grows linearly with poll volume. opcgw runs a background pruner that deletes rows older than the configured retention window. Pruning is logged as operation = "prune_metric_history"; the line carries the cutoff timestamp and the row count deleted.

listitem.para: The retention window is read from the same TOML used at startup; no environment override.
Pruning is throttled by an internal retry-back-off so transient SQLite contention does not delay the next poll cycle.
To disable pruning, set the retention to a value larger than your operational horizon (the pruner has no "off" setting; setting retention to a long window achieves the same effect).

section.title: Command History Retention
section.para: Completed commands (Confirmed or Failed) are retained for [command_delivery].history_retention_days. The pruner runs alongside the metric pruner; both share the same retry and back-off logic.

section.title: Log Rotation
section.para: All five log appenders are daily-rolling: at UTC midnight a new file is created with the date suffix. opcgw does not delete old log files automatically; manage retention with your platform's standard tooling (logrotate, journald, Docker's logging driver, or a cron job under opcgw.cron.d).

appendix.title: Configuration Reference
appendix.para: Quick-lookup table for every TOML key. See for narrative descriptions and examples.

appendix.title: OPC UA Address Space
appendix.programlisting: Objects/ └── opcgw/ ├── Gateway/ │ ├── LastPollTimestamp (String, RFC 3339 or NULL) │ ├── error_count (Int32) │ └── chirpstack_available (Boolean) └── <application_name>/ (one folder per [[application]]) └── <device_name>/ (one folder per [[application.device]]) ├── <metric_name> (Variant per [[..read_metric]].metric_type) └── <command_name> (writable, per [[..command]])
appendix.para: Every read returns a StatusCode. For uplink metrics it is derived from the metric's age (Good / Uncertain / Bad). For Gateway-folder reads it is always Good, except that LastPollTimestamp may be NULL (still reported with Good status — the NULL value itself is the signal).

appendix.title: Operation Names
appendix.para: The complete reference of operation = ... values, expected fields, level, and remediation tips lives in docs/logging.md. A condensed table appears in . When adding a new operation, register it in docs/logging.md first so the log-analysis side has a contract to honour.
Reserved operation names (planned for future stories, not yet emitted):

listitem.para: recovery_attempt, recovery_complete, recovery_failed — Story 4-4 auto-recovery from ChirpStack outages.
Open-source LoRaWAN Network Server that manages devices and collects metrics.
Sharded concurrent hash map used by opcgw's OPC UA server to track per-metric staleness state without serialising reads on a single mutex.
Globally unique 64-bit identifier for a LoRaWAN end-device, used as the device_id in opcgw configuration.
Synthetic OPC UA folder under the configured root containing the gateway's own health metrics (LastPollTimestamp, error_count, chirpstack_available).
Remote-procedure-call framework built on HTTP/2 used by ChirpStack's API.
Long-Range Wide-Area Network protocol for low-power IoT devices.
OPC Unified Architecture, a machine-to-machine communication standard for industrial automation.
Per-OPC-UA-read UUID v4 emitted as a tracing-span field; all log lines emitted while the span is active inherit it, enabling end-to-end correlation by simple grep.
Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition system for monitoring and controlling industrial processes.
Write-Ahead Logging journaling mode that allows multiple readers to operate concurrently with a single writer. opcgw enables WAL at startup; it is the foundation of the lock-free read path.
OPC UA per-read status indicator. opcgw maps metric freshness to Good, Uncertain, or Bad.
The Rust crate providing structured, span-aware logging. opcgw emits all log output through it.
Write-Ahead Log — see SQLite WAL mode.
